data.content_ref: inline:{"claim": "Palace systems collapsed through endogenous institutional fragility: Mycenaean wanax-centered redistribution, Hittite vassalage management, and Levantine commercial monopolies each developed coordination failures that Egyptian divine kingship and temple estates circumvented", "mechanism": "increasing returns to palace scale created brittle networks; Mycenaean Linear B confirms hypercentralized resource allocation; Hittite 'Great Kingship' required constant military validation; Ugarit's mercantile oligopoly suffered from single-commodity copper dependence; Egyptian dual temple-state structure permitted decentralized survival of Thebes and Saïs even when royal authority fragmented", "scope": {"covers": ["Mycenaean Greece", "Hittite Anatolia", "Ugarit", "Levantine city-states", "Egypt"]}, "excludes": ["Mesopotamia", "Cyprus"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Egyptian Third Intermediate Period shows comparable institutional fragmentation to Hittite or Mycenaean collapses", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Linear B tablets reveal diversified production and multiple regional centers, not pure monocentric redistribution", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Ugarit archives show private merchant activity outside palace control, undermining monopoly thesis", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Hittite collapse followed external usurpation at Tarhuntassa, not internal coordination failure", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "School-0 mechanism candidate; most criticizable for teleological institutionalism and post-hoc Egyptian exceptionalism"}
